summary:
An increase in systolic blood pressure by 30 mmHg indicates potential worsening of chronic hypertension but doesn't specifically point towards superimposed pre-eclampsia as conclusively as increased proteinuria, retinal hemorrhages, or thrombocytopenia do. \boxed{Increase in SBP only}

Agent1's reasoning (Round 2): The presence of significant proteinuria, visual disturbances due to retinopathy, and reduced platelet counts are strong indicators for diagnosing superimposed pre-eclampsia over existing chronic hypertension since these represent acute organ damage associated with this condition. Conversely, merely noting higher than usual diastolic pressures isn’t sufficient proof; it might be part of ongoing high blood pressure rather than indicating active disease progression into pre-eclamptic state.
\boxed{D} Summary focusing solely on critical aspects:

An elevation purely in diastolic blood pressure suggests possible uncontrolled chronic hypertension more so than definitive signs pointing toward superimposed pre-eclampsia such as proteinuria, ocular abnormalities, or decreased platelets.\boxed{Diastolic BP rise exclusively}. 

Note how I've condensed Agent1’s detailed explanations while maintaining core diagnostic criteria essential for identifying true cases versus those suggestive primarily from elevated baseline readings alone. This aligns closely yet succinctly within your requested format constraints regarding brevity & focus on clinically decisive factors distinguishing between options presented here.
